What Is Git Worktree? The Secret to Running Multiple AI Agents in One Project

Git Worktree is a Git 2.5 feature that lets you have multiple working directories under the same repo, each with a different branch checked out. No stashing, no switching — and it's perfect for running multiple AI Agents in parallel.

A lot of people are already using AI Agents to help with all kinds of work. But in real projects you've usually got several features in flight at once, which raises a question: "Can a project only run one AI Agent at a time?" This post walks through how to actually run multiple AI Agents in the same project simultaneously.
Let's skip the preamble and jump in. Say we're building a TodoList with four main features:

Normally we'd build the Add feature first, then Query, Update, Delete — so the AI Agent works on them one after another. That gets the job done, but if the Add feature is complex and takes a while, the next feature is just sitting and waiting.
So here's what I want to introduce:
It's a Git feature, shipped back in version

. The short version: it lets you have multiple working directories inside the same Git repo at the same time, which means you can run multiple AI Agents on the same project, each working on a different task.
Normally you'd init a Git project like this:
branch. From there you can use
If you already have a branch (
, say) and want to give it its own working directory:
: where to put the new working directory

# Example: create a worktree for the existing feature branch
git worktree add ../feature-worktree feature
# Example: create a worktree for the existing feature branch
Pattern 2: Create a new branch + worktree at the same time
If you want a new branch and a working directory for it in one command:
: where to put the working directory

# Example: create a new feature-new branch with its own worktree
git worktree add ../feature-new-worktree -b feature-new
# Example: create a new feature-new branch with its own worktree
If "working directory" is still a bit fuzzy, here's the simplest way to think about it: it's the project folder you see on your computer.

/Users/ray/
├── my-project/           ← Main project (main branch)
└── my-project-feature/   ← New working directory (feature branch)
at the same time. Think of it like the same project opened in two windows — each window can be on a different version, editing independently, without interfering with each other.
Even simpler: it's basically a copy of the project, except it shares the same underlying Git repository.
Let's try this out with the TodoList from earlier — one worktree per feature.

# Add feature worktree on a new feature-add branch
git worktree add ../todo-add -b feature-add
# Query feature
git worktree add ../todo-query -b feature-query
# Update feature
git worktree add ../todo-update -b feature-update
# Delete feature
git worktree add ../todo-delete -b feature-delete
# Add feature worktree on a new feature-add branch
After running these, you'll see four new folders next to your main project. In my case the layout is

/Users/rayxu/GitHub/
├── todolist-worktree/        ← Main project (main branch)
├── todo-add/                 ← Add worktree (feature-add branch)
├── todo-query/               ← Query worktree (feature-query branch)
├── todo-update/              ← Update worktree (feature-update branch)
└── todo-delete/              ← Delete worktree (feature-delete branch)
Now open four terminal windows, one per worktree:
to fire up an AI Agent and let it work on that specific feature.
Below I gave all four the same simple instruction: "create an xxx.js file for me":
If you go back into each working directory you'll see the files have been created — and the original
main folder is still untouched, because all the new files were created inside the worktrees.
So how do you merge all this work back into the main project? Commit inside the worktree, then merge back from the main repo with

# 1. Commit inside the worktree
cd ../todo-add
git add .
git commit -m "feat: add new todo feature"

# 2. Remove the worktree (frees the branch)
cd ../todolist-worktree
git worktree remove ../todo-add

# 3. Merge the branch
git merge feature-add
If you hit "fatal: refusing to merge unrelated histories", you'll need to add the
. This happens because normally you should have at least one initial commit in the main project (todolist-worktree) before creating any worktrees.
If you open the commit graph, you'll see it looks just like a regular PR — a branch fanning out, then merging back into main.

Create and switch to a new branch
Create a worktree from an existing branch
Create a new branch + worktree at the same time
Merge unrelated histories (fix for the refusal error)
